skill.name: bioservices
skill.description: Use BioServices to query UniProt, KEGG, ChEMBL, ChEBI, QuickGO, Reactome, and related bioinformatics services from Python workflows. It helps retrieve sequences, map biological identifiers, inspect pathways, and cross-reference compounds across public databases.
skill.summary: Query and combine public bioinformatics databases through BioServices workflows.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found the external command and weak-crypto alerts are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, database names, and scientific terms. The confirmed risks are legitimate helper scripts that contact public bioinformatics services and write user-selected result files, so publication is acceptable with a network and filesystem warning.

medium_findings.title: External Bioinformatics Service Calls
medium_findings.description: The helper scripts submit protein sequences, identifiers, compound names, and an optional BLAST email address to public BioServices-backed databases. This is expected for the skill, but users should treat submitted research inputs as data shared with third-party scientific services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct API calls are visible in scripts and match the documented BioServices purpose. The risk is data disclosure to external scientific services, not malicious exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Result File Writes
medium_findings.description: Command-line helpers write CSV, SIF, text, and detailed pathway files to paths supplied by the user or default output names. This is legitimate export behavior, but it can overwrite files if users choose unsafe paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are explicit and are tied to result export functions. No destructive deletion is present, but output paths are not restricted.

low_findings.title: Static Shell and Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby backtick, weak cryptography, C2 keyword, reconnaissance, and dynamic-constructor alerts occur in Markdown examples or ordinary bioinformatics vocabulary. No evidence found of shell execution, obfuscation, credential access, or malicious control flow in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines show Python documentation examples and GO category text, not executable shell or cryptographic logic. Targeted review did not find matching malicious semantics.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Bioinformatics Service Calls
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper scripts submit protein sequences, identifiers, compound names, and an optional BLAST email address to public BioServices-backed databases. This is expected for the skill, but users should treat submitted research inputs as data shared with third-party scientific services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct API calls are visible in scripts and match the documented BioServices purpose. The risk is data disclosure to external scientific services, not malicious exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Result File Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: Command-line helpers write CSV, SIF, text, and detailed pathway files to paths supplied by the user or default output names. This is legitimate export behavior, but it can overwrite files if users choose unsafe paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are explicit and are tied to result export functions. No destructive deletion is present, but output paths are not restricted.

content.value_statement: Bioinformatics projects often need data from several public databases with different APIs. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through BioServices workflows for protein, pathway, compound, and identifier analysis.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use BioServices instead of single-database tools or sequence manipulation libraries.
Guides UniProt protein search, sequence retrieval, and cross-database identifier mapping.
Supports KEGG pathway lookup, KGML parsing, and pathway interaction export workflows.
Provides compound cross-reference workflows across KEGG, ChEBI, ChEMBL, and UniChem.
Includes command-line helper scripts for batch ID conversion, pathway analysis, compound lookup, and protein workflows.
Documents common database codes, service methods, and workflow patterns for public bioinformatics resources.
limitations: Requires the BioServices Python package and internet access to public database services.
External services may rate limit, change formats, or return incomplete mappings.
BLAST workflows can take several minutes and require a valid email address.
The helper scripts are examples and do not validate every biological identifier or output path.

use_cases.title: Map protein identifiers for a research dataset
use_cases.description: Convert UniProt, KEGG, Ensembl, RefSeq, or GeneID values in batches and export mapping summaries.

use_cases.title: Inspect pathways for genes or organisms
use_cases.description: Retrieve KEGG pathway data, summarize pathway sizes, and export interaction files for later analysis.

use_cases.title: Cross-reference compounds across databases
use_cases.description: Find KEGG compounds and connect them to ChEBI and ChEMBL identifiers with basic compound metadata.

prompt_templates.title: Look up one protein
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the bioservices skill to retrieve the UniProt entry and FASTA sequence for P43403. Explain each step before running code.

prompt_templates.title: Convert a small ID list
prompt_templates.prompt: Use BioServices to map these UniProt IDs to KEGG identifiers: P43403, P04637, and P53779. Summarize unmapped IDs separately.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze organism pathways
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a KEGG pathway analysis for human using BioServices. Include output files for pathway summaries and interaction networks.

prompt_templates.title: Build a multi-database compound workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a BioServices workflow that searches a compound name in KEGG, maps it to ChEBI and ChEMBL, and reports missing mappings clearly.

output_examples.input: Map UniProt IDs P43403 and P04637 to KEGG.
output_examples.output: A concise mapping table with source IDs, target KEGG IDs, mapping status, and notes for any missing results.

output_examples.input: Analyze KEGG pathways for organism code hsa with a small limit.
output_examples.output: A pathway summary with pathway IDs, names, gene counts, interaction counts, and exported interaction file names.
A compound report listing the selected KEGG compound, available ChEBI and ChEMBL IDs, formula, mass fields, and missing data notes.

best_practices: Use small test batches before submitting large identifier lists to external services.
Confirm database code names and organism codes before running long workflows.
Write outputs to a dedicated project directory to avoid overwriting unrelated files.
anti_patterns: Do not submit confidential sequences, patient data, or unpublished compounds to public services without approval.
Do not assume every identifier maps one-to-one across databases.
Do not run broad pathway or BLAST jobs without rate limits and timeout expectations.

faq.question: What is BioServices used for?
faq.answer: BioServices provides Python access to many public bioinformatics databases through a consistent workflow.

faq.question: Does this skill need internet access?
faq.answer: Yes. Most workflows query public services such as UniProt, KEGG, ChEBI, ChEMBL, QuickGO, and NCBI BLAST.

faq.question: Can it process batch identifier files?
faq.answer: Yes. The included batch converter reads one identifier per line and writes mapping results to CSV.

faq.question: Can it replace Biopython?
faq.answer: No. Use BioServices for database access. Use Biopython for local sequence parsing, file formats, and sequence manipulation.

faq.question: Are BLAST jobs immediate?
faq.answer: No. BLAST jobs are asynchronous and may take several minutes before results are ready.

faq.question: Is this safe for private research data?
faq.answer: Use caution. The scripts send queries to third-party public databases, so confidential inputs require policy review first.
